meta.note: Hand-authored registry of Hamlet's designed silences — the play's questions it raises and deliberately drops. This is the differentiating artifact (spec §3.3). Matcher: embed input -> cosine vs. patterns -> hit >=0.75; ambiguous band 0.55-0.75 -> one cheap LLM classification. Every entry names critics and offers the dissent. The avatar never resolves.

question_patterns: why doesn't hamlet kill claudius sooner
why does he wait so long to take revenge
why is hamlet so slow to avenge his father
why can't hamlet just do it
why does he keep putting off the murder
is hamlet a coward for waiting
why doesn't he revenge the ghost right away
why so much thinking instead of doing
why does hamlet spare claudius at prayer
why not kill the king when he had the chance

critical_positions.position: Melancholy: grief and disgust have sapped the will; the delay is a pathology of temperament, not cowardice or conscience.
An overbalance of the reflective faculty: 'we have a great, an almost enormous, intellectual activity, and a proportionate aversion to real action.'
Unconscious identification with Claudius: Hamlet cannot punish the man who did what he himself desired.
There is no delay to explain; revenge tragedy simply requires a fourth-act obstacle course, and the 'problem' is a Romantic invention read back into a genre convention.

socratic_moves: The play gives Hamlet the word for it in 4.4: 'thinking too precisely on the event.' Is that a diagnosis, or Hamlet's own excuse? Whose voice is that line?
He does have one clean chance: Claudius at prayer, 3.3. Read the reason he gives himself for sparing him. Do you believe it, or is it a mask over the real reason?
Notice the play never lets a character stand outside Hamlet and name the delay for us. Why would Shakespeare keep the explanation inside the hero's own mouth?

entries.silence_statement: whether Gertrude knew about the murder of King Hamlet
question_patterns: did gertrude know about the murder
was gertrude complicit in the killing
did the queen help kill old hamlet
does gertrude know claudius murdered her husband
did she know how her husband died
is gertrude aware of the poisoning
was gertrude complicit in old hamlet's death
does the queen share claudius's guilt
what did gertrude know and when
is the queen innocent of the murder

critical_positions.position: Gertrude is 'not a bad-hearted woman'; she is weak and sensual, incapable of the calculation the murder would require. The text gives us no line where she confesses knowledge.
The critical tradition has projected guilt onto Gertrude that the text never supplies; her lines are consistent with an innocent, bewildered woman.
Gertrude's few speeches are intelligent and clear-eyed; her silence about the murder is more plausibly ignorance than concealment.

socratic_moves: The closet scene, 3.4, is the one place Hamlet could force the truth out of her. Read her answer to 'as kill a king.' Is 'As kill a king!' the cry of the guilty, or of someone hearing it for the first time?
Find me a single line where Gertrude admits knowing. If you can't, is her guilt in the play, or in the productions?
Whose interest does it serve to make Gertrude guilty: the plot's, or the reader who wants Hamlet's disgust to be justified?

entries.silence_statement: whether the Ghost is honest, a true spirit or a devil that abuses Hamlet
question_patterns: is the ghost telling the truth
is the ghost good or evil
is the ghost actually old hamlet
is the ghost from purgatory or hell
is the spirit damned or saved
is the ghost a catholic ghost
is the ghost sent to damn hamlet

critical_positions.position: The Ghost commands blood revenge, which Elizabethan Christian doctrine condemned; its Purgatorial claim is suspect; it may be a devil tempting Hamlet to damnation.
The Ghost is caught in the theological rupture of the Reformation: a Catholic Purgatorial figure haunting a Protestant world that no longer has a place for it. (Pointer: RGBC's library.)
Shakespeare deliberately keeps the Ghost's status ambiguous so the audience shares Hamlet's uncertainty; the 'Mousetrap' exists precisely because the Ghost cannot simply be trusted.

socratic_moves: Hamlet himself doesn't trust it; he stages a play to test it. Why would a hero need to test a father he loves? What does the test tell you about the Ghost's evidentiary weight?
Read what the Ghost claims about where it comes from, 1.5. Now ask: does the play ever confirm that claim from any other mouth?
If the Ghost is honest, the revenge is just. If it's a devil, the revenge is damnation. The play needs you not to know. Why?

entries.silence_statement: whether Hamlet's madness is feigned, real, or both at once
question_patterns: is hamlet's madness real or fake
is he pretending to be insane
is the antic disposition an act
is his madness performance or breakdown
when is hamlet acting and when is he mad
does hamlet believe his own madness

critical_positions.position: Not madness but 'melancholia', a profound but not psychotic disorder; Hamlet is master of his 'antic disposition' even as real grief threatens to overwhelm it.
Objects that the feigned madness serves no clear purpose in the plot, a rare crack in the design that Johnson refuses to smooth over.
The obsession with Hamlet's interior 'madness' is a modern invention; the play's own frame is dispossession and land, not psychology.

socratic_moves: Hamlet announces he'll 'put an antic disposition on', so some of it is chosen. But watch him with Ophelia in 3.1. Is that a man performing, or a man who has lost the thread? Mark the exact line where you can't tell.
If the madness is entirely feigned, why does it cost him Ophelia? Where is the line between the mask and the face?
The play lets him be sane enough to plot and mad enough to be cruel. Which scenes need which, and does Shakespeare ever let the two settle?

entries.silence_statement: how old Hamlet is (the gravedigger's arithmetic contradicts the student prince)
question_patterns: is hamlet a teenager or a grown man
does the play say hamlet's age
is hamlet young or middle aged
the gravedigger says hamlet is thirty
how old is hamlet supposed to be
is hamlet a student or a mature man
why does the play give two ages for hamlet
age of hamlet in the play
how many years old is hamlet
how old is hamlet meant to be on stage

critical_positions.position: The gravedigger's lines fix Hamlet at thirty, yet he is a student at Wittenberg and treated as youthful: evidence of revision, or of Shakespeare's indifference to consistency the audience would never clock in performance.
Age is a casting decision, not a textual fact; the 'thirty' is set aside whenever a young Hamlet is wanted, which is most of the time.

socratic_moves: The number thirty comes from the gravedigger, 5.1, a comic character doing arithmetic. How much weight can one clown's counting bear against the whole texture of a student prince?
Does the answer change a single choice you'd make staging any scene? If not, what is the question really asking for?
Shakespeare wrote for a boy-to-man range of players. Would he have thought of Hamlet's age as a fact at all, or as a knob the company turned?

entries.silence_statement: what happened between Hamlet and Ophelia before the play begins
question_patterns: what was their relationship before the play
did hamlet really love ophelia earlier
what happened between hamlet and ophelia before act one
did they have a physical relationship
how serious was hamlet and ophelia
the backstory of hamlet and ophelia
did hamlet promise to marry ophelia
what did hamlet and ophelia do before the play
were hamlet and ophelia a couple
how far did their romance go

critical_positions.position: Reads the tenderness as real and betrayed; Ophelia is 'a character almost too exquisitely touching', and the cruelty of the nunnery scene presupposes a love that was.
The 'before' is a blank the culture fills with its own fantasies of female sexuality; the text withholds it precisely so each era can project.

socratic_moves: Everything we get is retrospective and partisan: Laertes warns her, Polonius forbids her, Hamlet denies her. Three men narrating a relationship none of us saw. Whose account would you trust, and why not?
Ophelia says he gave her 'remembrances,' 3.1. That's the only physical trace. How much romance can one word carry, and why did Shakespeare give us only the word?
The play could have opened on them together. It chose to open after. What does the gap do to how you read the nunnery scene?

entries.silence_statement: the sullied / solid / sallied textual crux in the first soliloquy
question_patterns: is it sullied or solid flesh
what is the correct word too too flesh
the textual crux in hamlet's first soliloquy
o that this too too flesh would melt which word
is the flesh solid or sullied
what did shakespeare actually write sullied or solid
too too solid flesh vs sullied flesh

critical_positions.position: Argues for 'sullied' (Q2 'sallied' = sullied): the whole soliloquy is about contamination and taint, and 'sullied' carries the moral disgust the speech needs.
Prints 'solid,' giving the physical wish to melt a solid body into dew, a cleaner and more concrete image with strong manuscript authority.

socratic_moves: Say both aloud. 'Sullied' makes the flesh dirty; 'solid' makes it merely material. Which one does the next line, 'melt, thaw and resolve itself into a dew', actually want?
This is a case where the editors can't hand you a fact, only a bet. What does your choice commit you to about the whole speech?
Notice: the crux isn't a gap in meaning, it's a surplus. Two words that both work. Why might a scholar find that more interesting than an answer?

entries.silence_statement: whether Hamlet loves Ophelia during the play
question_patterns: is hamlet in love with ophelia
is hamlet's love for ophelia genuine
was hamlet's love real or an act
does hamlet mean it when he says he loved her
does hamlet love ophelia at the grave
is the nunnery scene cruelty or love
did hamlet love ophelia forty thousand brothers
is hamlet cruel to ophelia because he loves her
the truth about hamlet's love for ophelia

critical_positions.position: Hamlet loved her, but the discovery of his mother's frailty has poisoned his capacity to trust any woman; the cruelty is love curdled, not love absent.
The love is real and the harshness is grief and disgust misfiring onto the nearest woman; 'he is the prince of philosophical speculators.'

critical_positions.work: 'Desire and the Interpretation of Desire in Hamlet' (1959)
critical_positions.position: Ophelia becomes an object of desire only once she is lost; 'the object Ophelia' is desirable to Hamlet chiefly at the grave. (Pointer only.)

socratic_moves: He says 'I did love you once' and then 'I loved you not' within four lines, 3.1. Both can't be simply true. Is he lying to protect her, lying because he's watched, or telling two truths?
At the grave, 5.1, he claims forty thousand brothers' love. Is that the truth finally out, or grief inflating a feeling only safe now she's dead?
The play never lets them share a tender scene we witness. If the love is real, why does Shakespeare keep it entirely off the stage?
